questions.q: Which hook should you use to avoid recreating an object reference on every render?
options: useCallback
useMemo
useRef
useState
questions.explanation: useMemo memoizes the computed value itself, recomputing only when dependencies change. useCallback memoizes a function reference. useRef persists a mutable value without triggering re-renders. useState causes a re-render whenever the value changes.

questions.q: When does React call the cleanup function returned by useEffect?
options: Only when the component unmounts
Before every re-render
Before running the effect again and when the component unmounts
Only when dependencies change
questions.explanation: React runs the cleanup before applying the next effect (if dependencies changed) and when the component unmounts. This ensures stale event listeners, subscriptions, or timers are removed before a new effect instance starts.

questions.q: What is the key purpose of React's reconciliation algorithm?
options: Converting JSX to JavaScript
Efficiently computing the minimal set of DOM changes needed
Batching multiple setState calls
Managing the component lifecycle order
questions.explanation: Reconciliation compares the new virtual DOM tree with the previous one (diffing) and computes the minimal set of actual DOM operations needed. It uses heuristics: same-type elements update in place, different-type elements destroy and recreate, and the key prop helps match list items across renders.

questions.q: Why is the key prop important when rendering lists in React?
options: Keys are only used for styling list items
Keys help React identify which items have changed, been added, or been removed
Keys are required to pass props to child components
Keys determine the order in which list items are rendered
questions.explanation: Keys help React match array items with their corresponding DOM nodes across re-renders. Without stable keys, React may reuse DOM nodes incorrectly, causing state and form values to get mixed up between list items. Using array indices as keys can break component state if the list is reordered.

questions.q: What happens when you use useCallback without a dependency array?
options: The function is never memoized
The function is memoized forever and never updates
The function is recreated on every render, negating the memoization benefit
React throws an error
questions.explanation: Without a dependency array, useCallback has no dependencies, so the memoized function reference never changes. This can cause stale closures in child components. If you omit the dependency array intentionally to create a new function every render, useCallback provides no benefit.

questions.q: How does React Context help avoid prop drilling?
options: Context eliminates the need for any props in the entire application
Context allows you to pass values through a Provider, making them accessible to deeply nested consumers without passing through intermediate components
Context automatically optimizes all component renders
Context only works with functional components that use hooks
questions.explanation: Context provides a way to share state without explicitly passing props through every intermediate component. A Provider at a high level makes a value available to all consumers below it in the tree. However, context updates still cause all consumers to re-render, which can be a performance concern for frequently changing values.

questions.q: What is the main difference between state and refs in React?
options: State is stored in memory; refs are stored in the DOM
State triggers a re-render when it changes; refs do not
Refs can only be used in class components
State cannot be used with hooks
questions.explanation: Updating state causes the component to re-render, whereas updating a ref (via .current) does not trigger a re-render. Use state for values that affect the UI and refs for values that don't need to trigger renders, like tracking focus, managing timers, or storing mutable values.

questions.q: When should you use useMemo to memoize a value?
options: Always, to optimize every variable
When the computation is expensive and the component renders frequently, or when the value is passed as a dependency to child components
Only in the root component
Never, because it adds unnecessary complexity
questions.explanation: useMemo is beneficial when computation is costly or when the memoized value is a dependency for other hooks/effects to prevent unnecessary re-calculations. Over-using useMemo on cheap computations can actually hurt performance due to the memoization overhead. Premature optimization should be avoided.

questions.q: What is the purpose of useEffect dependencies array?
options: To specify which props the component accepts
To determine when the effect should run by tracking which values it depends on
To manage the order of component renders
To automatically clean up all state on unmount
questions.explanation: The dependency array tells React which values the effect depends on. If no values in the array change between renders, the effect does not run. An empty array means the effect runs once on mount. Omitting the array causes the effect to run after every render. Missing dependencies in the array can cause stale closures and bugs.

questions.q: Why do controlled components require onChange handlers in React?
options: React cannot access input values without onChange
Controlled components derive their value from state, so onChange must update state to reflect user input and keep the component in sync
onChange handlers prevent form submission
They are only necessary for validation
questions.explanation: In a controlled component, the input value is set by React state. Without an onChange handler to update state, the input would remain frozen at its initial value even when the user types. The handler bridges the gap between DOM user input and React state, making the component predictable and debuggable.
